title:
3969. Design a Live User Count System for a Streaming Platform
Hard
Design a system to track and display the live user c

transcript.text:
3969. Design a Live User Count System for a Streaming Platform
Hard
Design a system to track and display the live user count for a video streaming platform, similar to Hotstar. The system should be able to handle millions of concurrent users, provide near real-time updates, and be highly available and scalable.

Consider the following aspects:

User Activity Tracking: How would you track active users on various streams and pages?
Real-time Aggregation: How would you aggregate this data to provide a global or per-stream live user count with minimal latency?
Scalability and Performance: How would the system scale to handle a massive number of users and high request rates?
Data Consistency: What consistency guarantees would you aim for, and how would you achieve them?
Technologies: Discuss suitable technologies (e.g., message queues, caching, databases, real-time processing frameworks).
Fault Tolerance: How would the system gracefully handle failures of individual components?

blocks.md:
# 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale first, then sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, and then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — mainly how we count tens of millions of distinct viewers in real time without melting a database. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: an **exact** distinct count of tens of millions of concurrent viewers, updated every second, is brutally expensive — but nobody actually needs "59,184,203." They need "~59M, and it's climbing." **The whole design leans on the fact that an approximate, slightly-stale count is not just acceptable — it's the right product choice.**

**Functional (the core flows — "above the line"):**
- **Track presence:** a viewer watching a stream (or sitting on a page) is counted as "active" while they keep showing up; they drop off shortly after they leave or their network dies.
- **Per-stream live count:** anyone viewing stream X can see the live count of people watching stream X.
- **Global live count:** show the total concurrent users across the whole platform (the "59M watching now" banner).
- **Real-time updates:** the displayed count refreshes every few seconds without a manual reload.

**Below the line (out of scope):** exact billing/analytics counts (that's a separate offline pipeline), per-user "who is watching" lists, historical time-series dashboards, recommendation, video delivery/CDN itself, chat.

**Non-Functional:**
- **Scale:** handle ~60M concurrent users (Hotstar's real cricket peak), millions of presence signals per second, with hot streams holding the vast majority of all viewers on a *single* logical stream.
- **Approximate, not exact:** counts within ~1–2% of truth are fine. This is a deliberate accuracy SLA, not a bug.
- **Near real-time:** displayed count is at most ~5–10s stale (p99).
- **Availability:** 99.9%+ ("three nines"); the count is a non-critical overlay — it must *never* take down or block video playback.
- **Consistency:** eventual/approximate everywhere. No read-your-own-writes requirement — a user does not need to see themselves reflected in the count.
- **Cost-aware:** this is a free overlay on top of the real product (video). It must be cheap per user; we can't afford a database write per heartbeat.

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** distinct-counting is inherently approximate at this scale, so I'm explicitly trading exactness for cost — I'll call out the error bound (~2% with HyperLogLog) and revisit it in the ledger. Also, because we never persist per-user presence durably, a full region failure loses in-flight presence and the count dips briefly until heartbeats refill it — acceptable for a count, and I'll say so.

> 💬 "Before I design anything — the single most important framing here is that this is a *count*, not a bank balance. Approximate and a few seconds stale is completely fine, and that one fact unlocks an enormously cheaper design."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So functionally this is small — four flows. Track that a viewer is present, show a per-stream count, show a global count, and keep both refreshing in near real-time. The hard part is entirely non-functional: sixty million concurrent users, with most of them piled onto one cricket match, and a count that has to update every few seconds. The thing I want to lock in early is that we don't need an exact number — being within a percent or two and a few seconds behind is totally acceptable, and honestly it's the correct product decision. That single relaxation is what lets this design be cheap instead of insane."

# 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each one moves the design:

- **"How accurate does the count need to be — is ±2% okay?"** → This is *the* question. If exact, I'm forced into distributed sets and it gets 100x more expensive. I'll assume **approximate is fine**.
- **"How fresh — is 5 seconds of staleness okay, or does it need sub-second?"** → Decides whether I can batch/aggregate on an interval (cheap) or need true streaming (expensive). I'll assume **~5s is fine**.
- **"Do we count distinct *users*, or concurrent *sessions/devices*?"** → One user on phone + TV = 1 or 2? Affects whether I dedup by user_id or session_id. I'll assume **distinct user_id** (the harder, more interesting case — sessions is strictly easier).
- **"Is the global count just the sum of all streams, or a separate distinct count (same user on two streams)?"** → Affects whether I can simply sum per-stream counts. I'll assume **sum of per-stream counts is acceptable** for the global banner (double-counting a tiny fraction of multi-stream users is within our error budget).
- **"Company/level bar?"** → I'll target a **Meta/Hotstar-style bar**: front-load scale and the read/fanout hot-path, move fast to a concrete design. Tell me if it's Google (I'll go deeper on the distinct-counting algorithm trade-off) or a startup (I'll start with just Redis).

Assumptions I'll proceed with: ~60M peak concurrent; heartbeat-based presence with a ~30s interval; approximate distinct counts (HyperLogLog-grade, ~2% error); ~5–10s display staleness; counts are an overlay that must degrade gracefully and never block playback.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — approximate, ~5s fresh, distinct users, heartbeat presence. If you'd rather I treat any of these as strict — say, exact counts — tell me now, because it changes the core of the design. Otherwise I'll size it."

Now let me size the few numbers that actually drive decisions.

blocks.name: run_command

input.command:
cat > capacity.py << 'EOF'
peak_concurrent = 60_000_000      # ~60M concurrent viewers (Hotstar cricket peak)
hb_interval_s = 30                # client heartbeat every 30s

# 1) Heartbeat ingestion rate (the binding write number)
hb_per_s = peak_concurrent / hb_interval_s

# 2) Hot stream: one match holds most of the platform
hot_share = 0.9
hot_stream_viewers = peak_concurrent * hot_share
hot_stream_hb_per_s = hot_stream_viewers / hb_interval_s

# 3) Read/display fanout if we PUSHED count to every viewer each second
push_interval_s = 5
push_msgs_per_s = peak_concurrent / push_interval_s   # if individually pushed

# 4) Naive exact approach cost: store a SET of user_ids per stream in Redis
#    memory for the hot stream's set
bytes_per_userid = 8            # 64-bit id
naive_set_bytes = hot_stream_viewers * bytes_per_userid * 1.5  # +overhead

# 5) HyperLogLog memory per stream (fixed ~12KB regardless of cardinality)
hll_bytes_per_stream = 12_000
num_streams = 5000
hll_total = hll_bytes_per_stream * num_streams

# 6) A single Redis node tops out ~150K-200K ops/s
redis_ceiling = 150_000

def human(n):
    for unit in ['','K','M','B']:
        if abs(n) < 1000: return f"{n:,.1f}{unit}"
        n/=1000
    return f"{n:,.1f}T"

print("Peak concurrent users:        ", human(peak_concurrent))
print("Heartbeats/sec (global):      ", human(hb_per_s))
print("Hot-stream viewers (90%):     ", human(hot_stream_viewers))
print("Hot-stream heartbeats/sec:    ", human(hot_stream_hb_per_s))
print("Push msgs/sec (per-user push):", human(push_msgs_per_s))
print("Naive SET memory (hot stream):", f"{naive_set_bytes/1e9:.2f} GB")
print("HLL memory per stream:        ", f"{hll_bytes_per_stream/1000:.0f} KB")
print("HLL memory all 5000 streams:  ", f"{hll_total/1e6:.1f} MB")
print("Single Redis node ceiling:    ", human(redis_ceiling), "ops/s")
print("Redis nodes if hot stream hit one node:", f"{hot_stream_hb_per_s/redis_ceiling:.0f}x over")
EOF
python3 capacity.py

blocks.output:
Peak concurrent users:         60.0M
Heartbeats/sec (global):       2.0M
Hot-stream viewers (90%):      54.0M
Hot-stream heartbeats/sec:     1.8M
Push msgs/sec (per-user push): 12.0M
Naive SET memory (hot stream): 0.65 GB
HLL memory per stream:         12 KB
HLL memory all 5000 streams:   60.0 MB
Single Redis node ceiling:     150.0K ops/s
Redis nodes if hot stream hit one node: 12x over

blocks.md:
# 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) I'm deferring full estimation — let me just compute the handful of numbers that actually force a design decision. | Quantity | Value | Why it matters | |---|---|---| | Peak concurrent users | ~60M | The headline scale | | **Heartbeats/sec (global)** | **~2M/s** | The binding write rate — at 30s interval | | Hot-stream heartbeats/sec | ~1.8M/s | 90% pile onto ONE match → the hot-key problem | | Per-user push msgs/sec | ~12M/s | Why we must NOT push individually | | Naive Redis SET (hot stream) | ~0.65 GB + 1.8M writes/s | Why exact distinct sets don't scale | | **HyperLogLog per stream** | **~12 KB (fixed!)** | The unlock — count 54M distinct in 12 KB | | HLL, all 5000 streams | ~60 MB total | Trivially fits in memory | | Single Redis node ceiling | ~150K ops/s | The flip threshold | **QPS** *(queries per second — how many requests hit us each second)* and **TTL** *(time-to-live — how long a record stays "alive" before auto-expiring; here, how long a heartbeat counts as "present")* are the two terms I'll lean on. The number that forces everything: **~2M heartbeats/sec, with ~1.8M of them on a single hot stream.** A single Redis node caps around ~150K ops/s — so the hot stream alone is **~12x over** what one node can take. That single fact kills the naive "one counter per stream" design and forces two things: **aggregate at the edge before writing**, and **count probabilistically** (HyperLogLog) instead of storing real user-id sets. > 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives this whole design is about two million heartbeats a second at peak, and the brutal part is that ninety percent of them — call it 1.8 million a second — land on a single cricket match. One Redis node does maybe 150K ops a second, so that hot stream is twelve times over what any single node can handle. That tells me two things immediately: I can't write every heartbeat to a counter, I have to pre-aggregate at the edge, and I can't store actual user-id sets — a single stream's set is gigabytes — so I'll count approximately with HyperLogLog, which holds a 54-million distinct count in twelve kilobytes. Storage overall is a non-issue, it's megabytes; this problem is entirely about absorbing the write firehose." # 4. Core Entities A first-draft list of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model (§7) later: - **Viewer (presence signal)** — a user currently watching; expressed as a heartbeat, not a durable row. Identified by user_id (or session_id). - **Stream** — a single live video (a match, a show). Has an id and a current live count. - **Heartbeat** — a periodic "I'm still here" event: {user_id, stream_id, timestamp}. - **Stream Counter** — the aggregated approximate distinct-viewer count for one stream (a HyperLogLog sketch, plus a cached integer). - **Global Counter** — the platform-wide total, derived by summing/merging stream counters. > 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are simple — a viewer expressed as a heartbeat, a stream, and the counter state per stream plus a global rollup. I'm deliberately not making the viewer a durable database row; presence is ephemeral and expires on its own. I'll firm up the exact fields when I get to the data model." # 5. API / Interface Walking each functional requirement to its endpoint: **1. Track presence (heartbeat)** — serves "track presence." ``` POST /v1/heartbeat Body: { stream_id } # user_id comes from the JWT, NEVER the body Response: 204 No Content ``` - Why POST: it's a write/signal. Why thin: this fires from ~60M clients every 30s — the response carries no payload to keep it cheap. **Security:** the caller's identity comes from the session/JWT, never a client-supplied user_id, so nobody can inflate a count by spoofing ids. It's naturally **idempotent** — re-sending the same heartbeat within a window is a no-op (presence is set-based, not additive). **2. Per-stream count** — serves "per-stream live count." ``` GET /v1/streams/{stream_id}/count Response: { stream_id, count: 53900000, approx: true, as_of: } ``` - Why GET: pure read, cacheable. `approx:true` and `as_of` tell the client (truthfully) it's an estimate and how fresh it is. **3. Global count** — serves "global live count." ``` GET /v1/count/global Response: { count: 59800000, approx: true, as_of: } ``` **4. Real-time updates** — serves "real-time updates." Instead of clients polling hard, push deltas over a long-lived connection: ``` GET /v1/streams/{stream_id}/count/stream (Server-Sent Events) → event: count data: { count, as_of } # server pushes every ~5s ``` - Why **SSE** *(Server-Sent Events — a one-way, long-lived HTTP connection where the server streams updates to the client; lighter than WebSockets because the count flow is one-directional)*: we only push server→client, so we don't need full duplex. Clients that can't hold a connection fall back to polling the GET above (with a cache-friendly `Cache-Control`). > 💬 "Heartbeat in, count out. The one thing I'll defend hard: user identity comes from the token, not the request body — otherwise the count is trivially forgeable." > 🎙️ **Script:** "Four endpoints, one per requirement. Clients POST a thin heartbeat every thirty seconds — and critically the user id comes from their token, not the body, so the count can't be spoofed. Reads are simple cacheable GETs for per-stream and global count, and both honestly label themselves as approximate with an as-of timestamp. For live updates I'll use Server-Sent Events — a one-way streaming connection — because the count only flows server-to-client, with plain polling as a fallback." # 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) ## 6.1 "Track presence" The slice: absorb ~2M heartbeats/sec without writing each one to a datastore, and let presence expire on its own. The trick is **edge aggregation**: heartbeats land on a fleet of stateless **Ingestion nodes** behind the load balancer. Each node keeps an in-memory, per-stream **HyperLogLog sketch** *(a probabilistic structure that estimates how many distinct items it's seen using ~12 KB regardless of count — it replaces storing the full set of user-ids)* and just folds each heartbeat's user_id into the local sketch. No per-heartbeat database write at all. | Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | Load Balancer | L7 LB (Envoy/ALB) | Spreads 2M req/s across ingestion fleet; TLS termination | Another hop | | Ingestion nodes | Stateless service, in-memory HLL per stream | Pre-aggregate so we never write per-heartbeat; HLL = ~12KB/stream | Local count is partial (only users that hit THIS node) | | Presence TTL | Implicit via HLL window rotation | Auto-expiry — drop a viewer ~30–60s after last heartbeat, no deletes | Count lags reality by one window | ```mermaid flowchart LR C[Client apps]:::client -->|1 POST heartbeat| LB[L7 Load Balancer]:::svc LB -->|2 route| IN[Ingestion node]:::svc IN -->|3 fold user-id into local sketch| HLL[(In-mem HLL per stream)]:::store classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; ``` ↳ reuses existing: nothing yet — this is the foundation. 1. Client POSTs a heartbeat (user_id from JWT, stream_id in body) every ~30s. 2. LB routes it to any ingestion node — they're stateless and interchangeable. 3. The node folds the user_id into its **local** in-memory HLL for that stream. **State change:** that stream's local sketch now "knows about" this user. No DB write. Old windows rotate out, so a user who stops sending heartbeats silently disappears from the count after the TTL window. > 💬 "The heartbeat never touches a database. Each ingestion node just updates a tiny in-memory sketch — twelve kilobytes per stream — and presence expires by itself when the heartbeats stop." > 🎙️ **Script:** "Presence is the firehose, so the key move is: heartbeats never hit a datastore individually. They land on a stateless ingestion fleet, and each node folds the user id into an in-memory HyperLogLog sketch — about twelve kilobytes per stream no matter how many viewers. Each node only sees the slice of traffic the load balancer sent it, so its sketch is partial — that's fine, I'll merge them in the next slice. And because sketches rotate on a time window, a viewer who stops sending heartbeats just ages out of the count automatically; there are no deletes." ## 6.2 "Per-stream live count" The slice: turn the many *partial* per-node sketches into one accurate per-stream count. HyperLogLog's superpower is that sketches are **mergeable** — union two sketches and you get the distinct count of the union, with no double-counting of users who hit different nodes. | Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | Aggregator | Stateful service (or Redis HLL) | Merges per-node sketches every ~5s into one per-stream sketch | Adds a merge tick of latency | | Count cache | Redis (the merged HLL + an int count) | Fast reads, all streams' sketches fit in ~60MB | Approximate (~2% error) | | Count Read API | Stateless service | Serves GET count from cache | — | ```mermaid flowchart LR IN1[Ingestion node]:::svc -->|1 push local sketch /5s| AGG[Aggregator]:::svc IN2[Ingestion node]:::svc -->|1 push local sketch /5s| AGG AGG -->|2 merge sketches| RC[(Redis: per-stream HLL + count)]:::store RAPI[Count Read API]:::svc -->|3 read count| RC C[Client apps]:::client -->|4 GET count| RAPI classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; ``` ↳ reuses existing: Ingestion nodes (from §6.1), Client apps. 1. Every ~5s, each ingestion node ships its per-stream local sketch to the Aggregator. 2. The Aggregator **merges** all nodes' sketches per stream (a lossless HLL union) and stores the merged sketch + the derived integer count in Redis. **State change:** Redis now holds the authoritative-ish per-stream count. 3. The Count Read API reads the integer from Redis (sub-millisecond). 4. Client GETs the count. > 💬 "Because the sketches are mergeable, I can let each node count independently and just union them every five seconds — a user who bounced between two nodes is still counted once." > 🎙️ **Script:** "Now I turn partial sketches into a real per-stream number. HyperLogLog sketches merge losslessly — unioning two of them gives you the distinct count of the combined set — so every five seconds each ingestion node ships its sketch to an aggregator, which unions them per stream and writes one integer count into Redis. Reads are then just a Redis GET, sub-millisecond. The whole thing is approximate to about two percent, which we already agreed is fine." ## 6.3 "Global live count" The slice: the platform-wide banner. Two ways — and I'll pick the cheap one because our error budget allows it. | Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | Global rollup | Aggregator job (sum, or HLL union) | Either sum all per-stream counts, or union all sketches | Sum may double-count multi-stream users (tiny) | | Global cache | Redis key `global:count` | One hot key, read by everyone | Must be replicated to handle read fanout | ```mermaid flowchart LR RC[(Redis: per-stream HLL + count)]:::store -->|1 read all stream sketches| AGG[Aggregator]:::svc AGG -->|2 union sketches -> global| GC[(Redis: global count)]:::store RAPI[Count Read API]:::svc -->|3 read| GC C[Client apps]:::client -->|4 GET global| RAPI classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; ``` ↳ reuses existing: Aggregator, Redis, Count Read API, Client apps (from §6.2). 1. The Aggregator reads all per-stream sketches. 2. It **unions all sketches** into one global sketch → exact-distinct across the platform (no double counting), or, if cheaper, just sums the per-stream integers. I'd union the sketches since it's only ~5000 × 12 KB and gives a true global distinct count. **State change:** `global:count` in Redis updated every ~5s. 3–4. Read API serves it; clients GET it. > 💬 "For the global banner I union all the per-stream sketches rather than summing the counts — that way a user watching two streams is still counted once, and it costs almost nothing because it's only sixty megabytes of sketches total." > 🎙️ **Script:** "The global count is just a rollup. I'll union all the per-stream sketches into one global sketch every five seconds — that gives a true distinct count across the platform without double-counting someone on two streams, and it's basically free since all the sketches together are about sixty megabytes. That goes into a single Redis key that everyone reads." ## 6.4 "Real-time updates" The slice: get the refreshed count to ~60M viewers every few seconds **without** 12M individual pushes/sec. The answer is fan-out through a delivery tier with **single-flight** broadcasting — one value computed once, broadcast to many connections. | Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | Pub/Sub | Redis Pub/Sub or Kafka topic per stream | Aggregator publishes ONE count update per stream per tick | One more hop | | Delivery (SSE) nodes | Stateless gateways holding viewer connections | Each holds ~100K SSE connections, broadcasts the one value to all | Many connection servers | | CDN fallback | Edge-cached GET count (TTL ~5s) | Polling clients hit the edge, not origin — collapses read fanout | 5s staleness | ```mermaid flowchart LR AGG[Aggregator]:::svc -->|1 publish count /5s| PS[[Pub/Sub per stream]]:::async PS -->|2 one msg| D1[SSE delivery node]:::svc PS -->|2 one msg| D2[SSE delivery node]:::svc D1 -->|3 broadcast to all conns| C[Client apps]:::client D2 -->|3 broadcast| C C -.->|alt: poll| CDN[CDN edge cache 5s]:::ext CDN -.->|miss| RAPI[Count Read API]:::svc classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a; classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe; ``` ↳ reuses existing: Aggregator (§6.2), Count Read API (§6.2/6.3), Client apps. 1. Every ~5s the Aggregator publishes **one** message per stream to Pub/Sub: the new count. 2. Pub/Sub delivers that single message to each SSE delivery node that has subscribers for that stream. 3. Each delivery node **broadcasts** the one value to all its held connections (a node holding 100K viewers does 100K cheap socket writes from one received message — not 100K recomputations). 4. Clients that can't hold an SSE connection poll the CDN-cached GET (5s TTL), so polling load collapses at the edge instead of hitting origin. > 💬 "I never push 12 million distinct messages a second. The count is computed once, published once per stream, and the delivery tier fans that single value out to everyone holding a connection." > 🎙️ **Script:** "Delivery is a fan-out problem. I do not push twelve million individual messages a second. The aggregator publishes one count per stream every five seconds into pub/sub, and a tier of stateless SSE gateways — each holding maybe a hundred thousand connections — receives that single message and broadcasts the same value to all its viewers. Anyone who can't hold a streaming connection just polls a CDN-cached endpoint with a five-second TTL, so polling collapses at the edge and never reaches our origin." ### Final (high-level) The reconciled union of every component from §6.1–6.4: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Clients C[Client apps]:::client end subgraph Ingest[Write / ingest path] LB[L7 Load Balancer]:::svc IN[Ingestion nodes
in-mem HLL/stream]:::svc end subgraph Agg[Aggregation] AGG[Aggregator
merge + rollup]:::svc RC[(Redis
per-stream HLL + count)]:::store GC[(Redis
global count)]:::store end subgraph Deliver[Read / delivery path] PS[[Pub/Sub per stream]]:::async DLV[SSE delivery nodes]:::svc RAPI[Count Read API]:::svc CDN[CDN edge cache 5s]:::ext end C -->|1 heartbeat| LB --> IN IN -->|2 local sketch /5s| AGG AGG -->|3 merge| RC AGG -->|4 union| GC AGG -->|5 publish count| PS --> DLV DLV -->|6 broadcast| C C -.->|poll| CDN -.->|miss| RAPI RAPI --> RC RAPI --> GC classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe; ``` Load-bearing schema inline: Redis `stream:{id}:hll` (12 KB sketch) + `stream:{id}:count` (int); `global:count` (int) + `global:hll`. > 🎙️ **Script:** "End to end: heartbeats hit a load balancer and spread across stateless ingestion nodes, each keeping a tiny in-memory sketch. Every five seconds the aggregator merges those sketches into a per-stream count in Redis and unions everything into a global count. Then it publishes one update per stream into pub/sub, and the SSE delivery tier fans that single value out to all the viewers. Polling clients hit a CDN cache instead. The write path is all about absorbing the firehose with pre-aggregation; the read path is all about fanning out one number cheaply." > 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the full skeleton. The two richest deep dives are how we count distinct viewers approximately — the HyperLogLog choice — and how we survive the hot stream where one match is 1.8 million heartbeats a second. Which do you want first, or is there another corner you'd rather probe?" # 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Key fields | Store | Why (access pattern) | |---|---|---|---| | Heartbeat | user_id (from JWT), stream_id, ts | **None — ephemeral** | Never persisted; folded into in-memory HLL then discarded. Persisting 2M/s would be the whole cost. | | Per-stream sketch | `stream:{id}:hll` (12 KB) | **Redis** (in-mem KV) | Mergeable, fixed size, sub-ms read/write; ~60MB total. Approximate by design. | | Per-stream count | `stream:{id}:count` (int), `as_of` | **Redis** | Hot read; cached integer so reads don't recompute from sketch. TTL-refreshed each tick. | | Global count | `global:count`, `global:hll` | **Redis** (replicated) | One hot key read by everyone → needs read replicas / local caching. | | Stream metadata | stream_id, title, status | **Postgres** (small) | Low volume, relational, durable; not on the hot path. | Partitioning: shard Redis **by stream_id** so different streams live on different nodes. The hot stream is the exception (handled in §8). Consistency: **eventual/approximate everywhere** — no strong consistency anywhere on the count path; stream metadata is strongly consistent but tiny and off the hot path. > 🎙️ **Script:** "Storage choices follow the access pattern. Heartbeats are never persisted — that's the single most important decision; writing two million rows a second would be the entire cost of the system, and we don't need them. The per-stream sketches and counts live in Redis because they're tiny, mergeable, and need sub-millisecond reads; all five thousand streams fit in about sixty megabytes. The global count is one hot key, so I replicate it and cache it locally to survive the read fanout. The only durable, relational store is stream metadata in Postgres, and that's small and nowhere near the hot path." # 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great > 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the two anchors — "we only need approximate" and "1.8M heartbeats land on one stream" — and reason forward from there; every answer falls out of those two facts. ## How do we count distinct viewers without storing every user-id? #### Bad: a Redis SET of user_ids per stream **Approach:** `SADD stream:{id} user_id` on every heartbeat, `SCARD` to read. **Challenges:** The hot stream's set is **~0.65 GB** and takes **1.8M writes/sec** to one key — ~12x a single node's ceiling. ⚠️ **Trap:** this is the obvious first answer and it's a hard fail at this scale — exact distinct counting means O(N) memory and a write per heartbeat. It also can't expire cleanly (you'd need per-member TTLs Redis sets don't have). #### Good: sharded approximate counters **Approach:** N counters per stream across nodes; INCR on heartbeat, sum to read; use a dedup window so the same user isn't counted twice within a TTL. **Challenges:** Without storing user-ids you can't truly dedup, so you either over-count (every heartbeat increments) or need a per-user "seen recently" check — which reintroduces the set. Better than Bad, but accuracy is shaky. #### Great: HyperLogLog with edge-merge **Approach:** Each ingestion node keeps an in-memory **HyperLogLog** per stream (~12 KB). Heartbeats fold in (`PFADD`-style). Every 5s, union all nodes' sketches (`PFMERGE`-style) → one count per stream. **HyperLogLog** *(a probabilistic cardinality estimator: it hashes each id and tracks the longest run of leading zeros across buckets to estimate distinct count — fixed ~12 KB for any cardinality, ~2% standard error)*. **Trade-offs:** ~2% error (within our SLA) and you can't ask "is user X present" — but we never need that. This is what real systems (Redis HLL, BigQuery `APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT`, Hotstar's own counter) actually do. ```mermaid flowchart LR H[heartbeats]:::client --> N1[Node HLL]:::svc H --> N2[Node HLL]:::svc N1 -->|PFMERGE| U[(Union sketch)]:::store N2 -->|PFMERGE| U U -->|PFCOUNT| CNT[count ~54M in 12KB]:::svc classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; ``` > 🎙️ **Script:** "The right answer is HyperLogLog. Instead of storing every user-id, each node keeps a probabilistic sketch that estimates distinct count by hashing ids and tracking bit patterns — twelve kilobytes holds a fifty-four-million count to within about two percent. The sketches merge losslessly, so I count locally and union every five seconds. I give up the ability to ask 'is this specific user here,' but I never need that for a count." > 🧠 **If they ask "why not just an exact set?":** "An exact set is gigabytes per stream and a write per heartbeat — twelve times over a node's ceiling — and we don't need exactness. HLL trades two percent error for a thousand-fold memory and write reduction. That's the correct trade for a display count." ## How do we survive the hot stream (1.8M heartbeats/sec on one key)? #### Bad: one Redis key for the hot stream **Approach:** all heartbeats `PFADD` to `stream:hot:hll`. **Challenges:** Single-key, single-node → ~12x over ceiling. The hot key is the binding bottleneck. ⚠️ **Trap:** sharding *streams* across nodes doesn't help when 90% of traffic is *one* stream — you must shard *within* the stream. #### Great: in-process aggregation + sub-sharded merge **Approach:** The fix is already baked into the architecture — **never write per-heartbeat to Redis at all.** Heartbeats hit ~hundreds of stateless ingestion nodes; each holds its *own* local HLL for the hot stream entirely in process memory. 1.8M/s ÷ 300 nodes ≈ **6K folds/sec/node** — trivial, in-RAM, no Redis. Only the **merge** touches Redis: 300 nodes × one sketch every 5s = ~60 merges/sec. We turned 1.8M writes/sec into ~60. New ceiling: bounded by node count, which we scale horizontally. **Trade-offs:** count lags by one ~5s merge window; a node crash loses its in-flight partial sketch (~5s of one node's slice → sub-1% blip). ```mermaid flowchart LR HK[1.8M hb/s hot stream]:::client --> LB[LB spreads]:::svc LB --> A[Node local HLL]:::svc LB --> B[Node local HLL]:::svc LB --> Z[300 nodes]:::svc A -->|1 sketch /5s| M[(Redis merge ~60/s)]:::store B --> M Z --> M classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; ``` > 🎙️ **Script:** "The hot stream is the whole game — 1.8 million heartbeats a second on one logical key, twelve times a node's ceiling. Sharding by stream doesn't help because it's one stream. The fix is to never write per-heartbeat to Redis: spread the heartbeats across a few hundred stateless nodes, each folding into its own in-memory sketch — that's only about six thousand cheap in-RAM operations per node per second. The only thing that touches Redis is the merge, three hundred sketches every five seconds, about sixty operations a second. I turned 1.8 million writes a second into sixty." > 🧠 **If they ask "what if one stream is too big even for in-RAM sketches?":** "It isn't — a sketch is 12 KB regardless of viewers. The scaling axis is heartbeat *rate*, which I absorb by adding stateless nodes; the sketch size never grows." ## How fresh, and what's the consistency story? The count is **eventually consistent and approximate**. End-to-end staleness budget: heartbeat interval (up to 30s to *appear*) + merge tick (~5s) + publish/fanout (~1s) → a new viewer shows up within ~30s, count value refreshes every ~5s. **No read-your-own-writes** — a user does not expect to see themselves in "59M watching," so we explicitly don't provide it, which saves us from any synchronous path. Departures are detected by **TTL expiry**: when heartbeats stop, the user ages out of the sketch within one rotation window (~30–60s), so a mass exit (match ends) drains over ~a minute — acceptable and arguably nicer UX than an instant cliff. > 🎙️ **Script:** "Everything here is eventually consistent and approximate, on purpose. A new viewer appears within about thirty seconds, the number refreshes every five, and people leaving age out by TTL when their heartbeats stop. There's no read-your-own-writes requirement — nobody expects to spot themselves in a sixty-million count — and dropping that requirement means nothing on this path has to be synchronous." > 🧠 **If they ask "isn't TTL-based departure too slow?":** "For a display count, a smooth ~minute drain when a match ends is fine, even preferable to a jarring instant drop. If product wanted faster, I'd shorten the heartbeat interval at the cost of more ingestion traffic — a tunable knob, not a redesign." ## How does global read-fanout not melt one key? ~60M clients want the global number. If they all read one Redis key or poll origin, that key/origin is the new hot spot. **Relief:** (1) push, don't pull — SSE fans one published value to all connections; (2) for pollers, a **CDN edge cache** with 5s TTL collapses millions of polls into ~one origin fetch per edge per 5s (**single-flight** *(only one request per key is allowed through to origin at a time; others wait for its result)*); (3) the `global:count` key is read from Redis **replicas**, not the primary. > 🎙️ **Script:** "The global count is read by everyone, so it's its own hot spot. Three defenses: push the value over SSE so it's computed once and broadcast, cache it at the CDN with a five-second TTL so millions of polls collapse to roughly one origin fetch per edge, and serve it from Redis replicas. The read side mirrors the write side — compute once, fan out cheap." # 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost **Availability:** target **99.9%**. Crucially, the count is a **non-critical overlay** — every failure path degrades to "stale or hidden count," never "broken playback." Per-dependency graceful degradation: - **Ingestion node dies:** loses its ~5s in-flight partial sketch → sub-1% count blip; LB reroutes heartbeats instantly (stateless). - **Aggregator dies:** count freezes at last value; standby takes over in seconds; clients keep showing last `as_of` (labeled stale). - **Redis primary dies:** failover to replica; worst case count resets and refills from the next heartbeat window (~30s) — sketches are rebuildable from the live stream, so no durable loss matters. - **Pub/Sub down:** SSE stops updating; clients fall back to CDN polling. - **Whole region down:** geo-route viewers to another region; that region's count reflects its own viewers (per-region counts merged in a global aggregator). Brief dip, self-heals as heartbeats land. **RPO/RTO:** because all state is **rebuildable from the live heartbeat stream within ~30s**, RPO is effectively "one heartbeat window" and RTO is seconds — there's no precious durable data to restore. Stream metadata (Postgres) is the only thing backed up normally. **Cost (rough monthly):** dominated by the **ingestion + delivery compute** (holding 60M connections and absorbing 2M req/s) — call it a few hundred compute nodes — plus **CDN egress** for pollers. Redis is trivial (~60 MB). Dominant driver: **connection-holding/delivery fleet + CDN egress**, on the order of low-hundreds-of-thousands $/mo at peak, near zero off-peak (autoscaled to live events). **Observability:** SLO burn-rate alerts on count freshness (`as_of` age) and merge lag; distributed tracing on the heartbeat→count path; a dashboard comparing approximate count vs a periodic exact sample to watch HLL error. > 🎙️ **Script:** "The reliability story is easy because the count is disposable — all state rebuilds from the live heartbeat stream within about thirty seconds, so there's nothing precious to restore; RPO is one heartbeat window, RTO is seconds. Every failure degrades to a stale or hidden count, never to broken video. A node loss is a sub-one-percent blip, an aggregator loss freezes the number at its last labeled-stale value, and a Redis failover just refills from the next window. Cost is dominated by the connection-holding delivery fleet and CDN egress, and it autoscales down to almost nothing between live events." # 10. Trade-off Ledger | Decision | What I gave up | What would reverse it | |---|---|---| | **HyperLogLog (approximate)** | Exactness (~2% error) and per-user presence queries | If product needed exact counts (e.g. for payouts/regulatory), I'd move to an exact pipeline with sampling caps — but that's a different, far costlier system. Reverses if the SLA becomes "exact." | | **TTL/heartbeat-based departure (~30–60s lag)** | Instant departure detection | If sub-second presence mattered (e.g. interactive game lobby), I'd use persistent WebSocket connection state as the source of truth — reverses if heartbeat overhead or lag becomes unacceptable. | | **Sum/union per-stream → global (eventual)** | Strong global consistency | At a smaller scale you could keep an exact global set; reverses if concurrency dropped ~100x and exactness were required. | These all tie back to the opening SLOs (approximate, ~5–10s fresh, 99.9%). The one I'd flag from the opener: the brief count dip on region failure — acceptable for a display metric, and I'd note it explicitly rather than over-engineer multi-master presence. > 🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'm least dogmatic about: choosing approximate HyperLogLog over exact counts, using TTL-based departure which lags by up to a minute, and rolling up to a global count eventually rather than strongly. All three are correct *because* this is a display metric — and all three flip the moment the requirement becomes 'exact' or 'sub-second,' which would be a genuinely different system, not a tweak." # 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **1. Why HyperLogLog over an exact set?** At 1.8M heartbeats/sec on the hot stream, an exact set is ~0.65 GB on one key with a write per heartbeat — 12x over a Redis node's ceiling. HLL is a fixed 12 KB regardless of cardinality with ~2% error, and its sketches merge losslessly so we can aggregate at the edge. We give up exactness and per-user lookup, neither of which a display count needs. > 💬 "Exact is gigabytes and a write per heartbeat; HLL is twelve kilobytes and two percent error. For a count, that's a no-brainer trade." **2. How do you handle the one match with 90% of all viewers?** Sharding by stream is useless when it's one stream, so I shard *within* the stream: heartbeats spread across hundreds of stateless nodes, each folding into its own in-memory sketch (~6K ops/node/sec), and only the 5-second merge touches Redis (~60 ops/sec total). The 1.8M-write firehose becomes ~60 merges/sec. > 💬 "I don't shard the stream across nodes, I shard the *traffic* — every node counts a slice locally, and I only merge sixty sketches every five seconds." **3. How do you detect someone leaving?** Presence is heartbeat-driven with a TTL window; when heartbeats stop, the user ages out of the rotating sketch within ~30–60s. There's no explicit "leave" event to lose. A match ending drains the count smoothly over ~a minute, which is fine — arguably better UX than an instant cliff. > 💬 "Leaving is just the absence of heartbeats — they age out of the window automatically, no delete needed." **4. What's the staleness, and is that okay?** A new viewer appears within ~30s (heartbeat interval), the value refreshes every ~5s (merge + publish), so worst-case the number is ~5–10s behind truth. For a "watching now" banner that's imperceptible, and we label every response with `as_of` so clients know the freshness. > 💬 "Five to ten seconds stale, and we're honest about it with an as-of timestamp — perfect for a display count." **5. How do you get the count to 60M clients without 12M messages/sec?** Push, don't pull: the aggregator publishes one count per stream into pub/sub every 5s; SSE delivery nodes each hold ~100K connections and broadcast that single value to all of them. Pollers hit a CDN cache with a 5s TTL, collapsing millions of polls to ~one origin fetch per edge. > 💬 "Compute once, publish once, fan out cheap — and CDN-cache the pollers so they never reach origin." **6. What if an ingestion node crashes mid-window?** It loses only its in-flight ~5s partial sketch — one node's slice of traffic for one window, well under 1% of the count — and the LB instantly reroutes its heartbeats since nodes are stateless. The next merge self-corrects. > 💬 "We lose one node's five-second slice — a sub-one-percent blip that heals on the next merge." **7. Why not WebSockets instead of SSE?** The count only flows server→client, so we don't need WebSocket's full duplex; SSE is lighter, works over plain HTTP, and auto-reconnects. If we later added interactivity (reactions, chat) I'd revisit WebSockets, but for a one-way count SSE is the leaner fit. > 💬 "It's a one-way stream, so SSE — full-duplex WebSockets would be paying for a feature we don't use." **8. How do you prevent count inflation/abuse?** User identity comes from the JWT, never the request body, so a client can't spoof other user-ids; HLL dedups by hashed user_id so a single user spamming heartbeats still counts once; and we rate-limit heartbeats per connection at the edge. Bot armies would need real distinct authenticated accounts to move the number. > 💬 "Identity is from the token and HLL dedups by user, so spamming heartbeats can't inflate the count — you'd need real distinct accounts." **9. Single point of failure in the aggregator?** The aggregator is a leader-elected role with hot standbys; if it dies the count simply freezes at the last value (clients show it as stale) until a standby takes over in seconds. Since all state is rebuildable from the live heartbeat stream, the standby just starts merging again — nothing to recover. > 💬 "Aggregator is leader-with-standby; if it dies the count freezes briefly, then a standby resumes — there's no state to restore." **10. How would you make the count exact if forced to?** I'd switch the hot path to durable session tracking (each connect/disconnect as an event into Kafka, deduped by session, with a stream processor like Flink maintaining exact distinct counts) — far costlier in storage and compute, and slower. I'd only do it if exactness were a hard requirement (billing, regulatory), and I'd cap it with sampling for the display layer. > 💬 "Exact means durable connect/disconnect events through Kafka and Flink — doable, but ten-times the cost, so I'd only do it if exactness were truly required." **11. How does this work multi-region?** Each region runs its own ingestion + aggregation and computes a regional count; a top-level aggregator unions the regional sketches (HLL union is cross-region-safe) for the global number. Viewers are geo-routed to their nearest region, and a region failure just drops that region's contribution until traffic re-routes and refills. > 💬 "Each region counts itself, and because HLL sketches union losslessly, the global count is just the union of regional sketches." **12. How do you keep Redis from being the bottleneck?** Redis only ever sees merges (~60/sec) and replica reads, never per-heartbeat writes — all the heavy lifting is in-process on ingestion nodes. Sketches are sharded by stream across the cluster, and the hot global key is read from replicas plus cached at the CDN. > 💬 "Redis never touches a raw heartbeat — it only stores merged sketches, so it's doing dozens of ops a second, not millions." **13. What about a "thundering herd" when a big match starts?** Millions connect within seconds; I stagger heartbeat timing with client-side jitter so they don't all fire on the same tick, autoscale the ingestion and SSE fleets ahead of scheduled events (we know match times), and the CDN absorbs the read surge. The HLL/merge cost is flat regardless of how fast viewers arrive. > 💬 "We pre-scale for scheduled matches and add client jitter so heartbeats don't synchronize — and the counting cost is flat no matter how fast people pour in." **14. How would you extend this to per-region or per-device-type breakdowns?** Keep separate HLL sketches keyed by the dimension (stream×region, stream×device); each is still 12 KB and merges independently, so I can show "X watching on mobile in India" by maintaining a sketch per bucket. The cost scales with number of buckets, not viewers, so it stays cheap. > 💬 "Just keep a separate twelve-kilobyte sketch per breakdown — region, device — they all merge independently, so adding dimensions is nearly free." > 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a count, not a bank balance — approximate and a few seconds stale is exactly right, and that unlocks everything. We're at sixty million concurrent users sending two million heartbeats a second, with 1.8 million of them on a single cricket match. So: heartbeats never touch a database — they hit a stateless ingestion fleet where each node folds the user into an in-memory HyperLogLog sketch, twelve kilobytes that estimates distinct count to within two percent and merges losslessly. Every five seconds we union the sketches into a per-stream count in Redis and a global count, then publish one value per stream into pub/sub, and an SSE delivery tier fans that single number out to all sixty million viewers — pollers hit a CDN cache. The hot stream stops being scary because we shard the traffic, not the stream: 1.8 million writes a second becomes sixty merges a second. Everything's eventually consistent, all state rebuilds from the live stream in thirty seconds so there's nothing to restore, and every failure degrades to a stale count, never broken video."
